title: Cold Symptom Self-Care
content: Common cold patterns often include a runny nose, sneezing, sore throat, and mild cough. Supportive care usually centers on hydration, rest, and symptom monitoring. Worsening fever or any breathing difficulty should trigger clinical review.
escalation: Seek clinician review if symptoms worsen after several days or breathing becomes difficult.

title: Influenza Monitoring Basics
content: Influenza often starts suddenly with fever, chills, body aches, headache, and fatigue. Patients should monitor their temperature, fluid intake, and breathing comfort. Medical review is important if fever persists, fluids cannot be maintained, or breathing worsens.
escalation: Persistent fever, dehydration, or worsening breathing should be evaluated promptly.

title: COVID-19 Home Monitoring
content: COVID-19 can include fever, cough, fatigue, loss of smell, loss of taste, and shortness of breath. People with respiratory symptoms should reduce close contact with others and watch for red-flag symptoms. Chest pain, low oxygen concerns, or confusion are signs to seek urgent care.
escalation: Chest pain, confusion, or shortness of breath need urgent assessment.

title: Hydration During Gastrointestinal Illness
content: Vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramps can quickly cause dehydration. Oral rehydration with small frequent sips is a common first step. Inability to keep fluids down, dizziness, or reduced urination should prompt medical review.
escalation: Dehydration, severe pain, or persistent vomiting requires prompt clinician review.

title: Migraine Trigger and Recovery Tips
content: Migraine episodes may include one-sided headache, nausea, light sensitivity, and dizziness. Resting in a dark quiet room and staying hydrated can help reduce discomfort. Sudden severe headache or neurological symptoms should not be managed as a routine migraine.
escalation: Sudden severe headache, weakness, or confusion warrants urgent evaluation.

title: Managing Allergy-Driven Nasal Symptoms
content: Allergic rhinitis commonly causes sneezing, runny nose, congestion, and itchy eyes. Reducing exposure to allergens and irritants can improve comfort. Wheezing or breathing difficulty suggests a more serious problem than simple nasal allergy symptoms.
precautions: Track pattern after pollen or dust exposure
escalation: Wheezing or breathing difficulty should be clinically assessed.

title: Bronchitis Warning Signs
content: Bronchitis may present with cough, chest discomfort, fatigue, and sometimes wheezing. Supportive care includes hydration and avoiding smoke, but worsening chest pain or shortness of breath can indicate a more serious lower respiratory issue. Persistent fever may also need clinician review.
escalation: Chest pain, persistent fever, or shortness of breath deserves prompt medical evaluation.

title: Urinary Infection Escalation Advice
content: Burning urination and frequent urination often suggest urinary irritation or infection. Fever, flank pain, or back pain can indicate a more advanced infection pattern. Early medical review helps reduce the risk of progression.
escalation: Fever with back pain should be assessed promptly.

title: High Blood Sugar Symptom Pattern
content: High blood sugar can cause frequent urination, excessive thirst, fatigue, and blurred vision. When urinary frequency occurs together with elevated glucose, the pattern is more suggestive of a glucose regulation issue than a simple urinary infection, especially if burning urination is absent. Persistently high readings should be medically reviewed.
precautions: Check glucose if you monitor at home
escalation: Vomiting, confusion, severe weakness, or very high glucose readings need urgent assessment.

title: Fever and Rash Monitoring
content: Fever with headache, body pain, nausea, and rash can occur in mosquito-borne viral illnesses. Hydration and close monitoring are important, especially when vomiting or abdominal pain appears. Bleeding, severe weakness, or persistent vomiting are red flags.
escalation: Bleeding, severe abdominal pain, or persistent vomiting need urgent evaluation.

title: Pneumonia Risk Overview
content: Pneumonia commonly causes fever, cough, chest pain, fatigue, and shortness of breath. Breathing difficulty and chest discomfort can signal higher clinical risk. Prompt evaluation is important when breathing work increases or confusion develops.
escalation: Breathing difficulty, chest pain, or confusion requires urgent care.

title: General Respiratory Red Flags
content: Any illness with chest pain, increasing shortness of breath, bluish lips, or confusion should be treated as potentially urgent. Mild cough or sore throat can be monitored, but escalating respiratory effort needs rapid medical assessment.
escalation: Urgent in-person care is advised for red-flag breathing symptoms.

title: Dehydration Prevention Guide
content: Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and poor oral intake can all cause dehydration. Monitor thirst, urine output, dizziness, and the ability to keep fluids down. Reduced urination or severe weakness increases urgency.
escalation: Severe dizziness, weakness, or inability to keep fluids down requires medical review.

title: Persistent Fever Follow-up
content: Fever lasting several days, especially when paired with worsening fatigue or breathing symptoms, deserves clinical attention. Symptom diaries help identify whether illness is improving or progressing.
escalation: Persistent fever beyond three days should prompt clinician review.

title: Headache Safety Rules
content: Headaches linked to dehydration, migraine, or viral illness are common, but thunderclap headache, weakness, or confusion are not routine. Severe neurological symptoms should always be assessed urgently.
escalation: Sudden severe headache or neurological symptoms require urgent assessment.

title: Allergy and Irritant Prevention
content: Sneezing, congestion, and itchy eyes often worsen with dust, pollen, smoke, or pets. Environmental control measures can reduce symptom burden, but wheezing or breathing trouble should be treated more seriously than routine allergy symptoms.
escalation: Breathing difficulty should not be managed as simple allergy symptoms.

title: When High Glucose Becomes Urgent
content: Persistent high blood sugar with increasing thirst, vomiting, confusion, deep rapid breathing, or fruity-smelling breath should be treated as urgent. Mild urinary frequency alone may not be an emergency, but combined metabolic warning signs require prompt in-person care.
precautions: Do not ignore worsening fatigue or vomiting
escalation: Confusion, vomiting, deep breathing, or very high glucose requires urgent medical evaluation.

title: Left Upper Abdominal or Spleen-Area Pain
content: Pain around the left upper abdomen, under the left ribs, or near the spleen has several possible causes and should not be forced into an unrelated disease label. If this pain persists, worsens, or follows trauma, clinician evaluation is appropriate. Fever, vomiting, fainting, or severe tenderness increase concern.
precautions: Avoid delaying care if pain intensifies
escalation: Urgent assessment is needed for severe pain, trauma, fainting, vomiting, or worsening abdominal tenderness.

title: When a Symptom Pattern Is Unclear
content: If a symptom description does not match a known low-risk pattern, the safest response is to avoid pretending certainty. Persistent localized pain, unusual organ-related discomfort, or symptoms that are hard to describe should be reviewed by a clinician rather than guessed at from a limited symptom checker.
precautions: Write down the symptom location and duration
Look for fever, vomiting, breathing pain, or trauma
Seek medical review for persistent or unusual pain
escalation: Escalate promptly if pain becomes severe or other warning signs appear.

title: Sinus Pressure and Congestion Advice
content: Sinusitis often causes congestion, runny nose, facial pain or pressure, headache, and sometimes sore throat. Most cases need symptom monitoring, hydration, and nasal comfort measures rather than emergency care. Persistent or severe symptoms deserve clinician review.
precautions: Track worsening facial pain or fever
escalation: Eye swelling, severe headache, or high fever requires prompt assessment.

title: Asthma Flare Warning Signs
content: Asthma flare patterns can include wheezing, cough, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. Any worsening breathing pattern should be taken seriously, especially if speaking becomes difficult. Rescue treatment plans help, but persistent breathlessness needs urgent care.
escalation: Difficulty speaking, worsening wheeze, or severe breathlessness needs urgent evaluation.

title: Kidney Stone Symptom Pattern
content: Kidney stones often cause severe flank or back pain and may be associated with nausea, vomiting, burning urination, or blood in urine. The combination of back pain and blood in urine should not be treated as a simple strain. Severe pain or inability to tolerate fluids needs urgent review.
escalation: Severe pain, fever, or inability to pass urine requires urgent care.

title: Upper Stomach Irritation Guidance
content: Gastritis or acid peptic irritation can cause upper abdominal pain, nausea, bloating, poor appetite, and heartburn. Symptoms often worsen after irritant foods, alcohol, or some medicines. Vomiting blood, black stools, or intense abdominal pain are not routine and need urgent assessment.
escalation: Black stools, vomiting blood, or severe abdominal pain needs urgent evaluation.

title: Right Lower Abdominal Pain Safety Note
content: Right-lower-abdominal pain with nausea, vomiting, fever, or appetite loss can reflect appendicitis and should not be dismissed. Worsening localized abdominal pain deserves prompt in-person evaluation. Delay is risky when the pain is increasing or movement makes it worse.
escalation: Increasing right-lower-abdominal pain or fever needs urgent evaluation.

title: Ear Pain and Fever Advice
content: Ear infections can cause localized ear pain, fever, headache, and sometimes throat discomfort. Persistent pain, hearing changes, or high fever should be clinically reviewed. Severe swelling around the ear is more concerning than simple mild pain.
escalation: High fever, severe ear pain, or swelling around the ear needs prompt assessment.

title: Prolonged Sore Throat and Fatigue
content: A mononucleosis-like illness may cause sore throat, marked fatigue, fever, headache, and swollen glands. When throat and fatigue symptoms last longer than a simple cold, clinician review can be helpful. Trouble swallowing or breathing is not routine and needs urgent care.
escalation: Breathing difficulty, severe throat swelling, or worsening abdominal pain needs urgent evaluation.

title: Mechanical Back Pain Overview
content: Musculoskeletal back strain commonly causes back pain, stiffness, or aching after lifting, posture strain, or overuse. It is less suggestive of urinary infection when burning urination, fever, or urinary frequency are absent. Weakness, numbness, or trauma are important warning signs.
escalation: Back pain with weakness, numbness, trauma, or fever needs medical review.
